skill.name: privilege-escalation-methods
skill.description: Reference documentation for privilege escalation techniques on Linux and Windows systems, intended for authorized penetration testing and red team operations.
skill.summary: Comprehensive privesc techniques including sudo abuse, SUID exploitation, token impersonation, and Active Directory attacks for security professionals.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill documents offensive security techniques for authorized penetration testing. Static analysis detected 125 patterns including shell execution, credential harvesting, and privilege escalation commands. All findings are TRUE POSITIVES representing documented attack techniques. The skill is educational reference material but contains actionable exploit instructions that require careful handling. Recommended for security professionals only with appropriate warnings.

critical_findings.title: Credential Theft Techniques Documented
critical_findings.description: The skill contains detailed instructions for harvesting credentials including Mimikatz usage, NTLM relay attacks, and Kerberos ticket manipulation. These are powerful offensive techniques that can compromise entire domains if misused.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: Explicit credential theft commands documented including DCSync, Golden Ticket creation, and NTLM hash extraction - confirmed offensive security techniques.

critical_findings.title: Privilege Escalation Exploit Code
critical_findings.description: Complete exploit chains for Linux (sudo abuse, SUID binaries, capabilities) and Windows (token impersonation, service abuse, driver exploitation) that grant root/administrator access.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct exploitation commands with verified GTFOBins techniques and Windows privilege escalation tools like SweetPotato and PowerUp.

high_findings.title: Active Directory Attack Chain
high_findings.description: Complete AD compromise workflow including Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, Golden Ticket creation, and Pass-the-Ticket attacks that can lead to full domain control.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Documented AD attack techniques using industry-standard tools (Rubeus, Mimikatz, Impacket) with specific command syntax.

high_findings.title: LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning
high_findings.description: Network credential harvesting using Responder and NTLM relay attacks that capture and crack Windows authentication hashes.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard network attack techniques with explicit tool usage and command examples.

high_findings.title: Persistence Mechanisms
high_findings.description: Scheduled task creation for persistent access using Golden Ticket authentication to maintain domain control.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Schtasks command with remote execution and PowerShell download cradle for persistent backdoor.

medium_findings.title: SUID Binary Exploitation
medium_findings.description: Techniques for creating and abusing SUID binaries to escalate from user to root on Linux systems.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: chmod +s commands create setuid root binaries for privilege escalation.

medium_findings.title: Capability Abuse
medium_findings.description: Exploitation of Linux capabilities (cap_setuid, cap_dac_read_search) to bypass traditional permission models.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct capability exploitation using getcap enumeration and Python/Perl privilege escalation.

medium_findings.title: NFS Root Squash Bypass
medium_findings.description: Abusing NFS no_root_squash configuration to create privileged binaries on mounted shares.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: NFS mount exploitation technique with SUID binary creation on remote share.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Commands
low_findings.description: Information gathering commands for enumerating sudo permissions, cron jobs, and system configuration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard enumeration commands (sudo -l, getcap, showmount) that are preparatory to exploitation.

dangerous_patterns.title: GTFOBins Technique Reference
dangerous_patterns.description: Comprehensive list of exploitable binaries (vim, find, awk, python, perl, less, man) that can spawn privileged shells when misconfigured in sudoers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Well-documented privilege escalation patterns from GTFOBins repository.

dangerous_patterns.title: Kerberos Attack Toolkit
dangerous_patterns.description: Complete Kerberos attack suite including ticket extraction, forgery, and replay attacks for domain compromise.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Industry-standard AD attack techniques using Mimikatz and Rubeus.

content.value_statement: Security professionals need reliable reference material for authorized penetration testing. This skill provides documented escalation techniques for Linux and Windows environments with proper context and prerequisites.
actual_capabilities: Document Linux privilege escalation via sudo misconfiguration, SUID binaries, and capability abuse
Provide Windows escalation techniques including token impersonation and service abuse
Reference Active Directory attack methods like Kerberoasting and Golden Ticket creation
Guide credential harvesting using LLMNR poisoning and NTLM relay attacks
Explain persistence mechanisms through scheduled tasks and privilege escalation
Offer enumeration commands for identifying escalation vectors on target systems
limitations: Requires initial shell access to target system before escalation techniques apply
Techniques are environment-specific and may not succeed on all systems
Tools mentioned (Mimikatz, Rubeus, PowerUp) must be obtained and deployed separately
Not suitable for production systems without explicit written authorization

use_cases.title: Penetration Tester Post-Exploitation
use_cases.description: Security consultant with initial foothold needs to demonstrate privilege escalation risks to client
use_cases.target_user: Penetration tester performing authorized security assessment

use_cases.title: Red Team Domain Compromise
use_cases.description: Red team operator needs reference for Active Directory attack chains during engagement
use_cases.target_user: Red team member simulating advanced persistent threat

use_cases.title: Security Research Education
use_cases.description: Defensive security researcher studying attack techniques to improve detection capabilities
use_cases.target_user: Blue team analyst developing threat detection rules

prompt_templates.title: Basic Enumeration
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a low-privilege shell on a Linux target. Show me enumeration commands to identify potential privilege escalation vectors including sudo permissions, SUID binaries, and writable cron jobs.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial reconnaissance on compromised Linux system

prompt_templates.title: Sudo Exploitation
prompt_templates.prompt: I found that my user can run vim as root without a password. Provide the GTFOBins technique to escalate to root using this misconfiguration.

prompt_templates.title: Active Directory Kerberoasting
prompt_templates.prompt: I have domain user credentials and need to identify service accounts vulnerable to Kerberoasting. Show me the Impacket and Rubeus commands to request and crack service tickets.

prompt_templates.title: Windows Token Impersonation
prompt_templates.prompt: I have SeImpersonatePrivilege on a Windows host. Explain how to use SweetPotato or SharpImpersonation to escalate to SYSTEM level access.
prompt_templates.scenario: Windows privilege escalation via token manipulation

output_examples.input: Enumerate sudo permissions on compromised Linux host
output: Review listed commands that can be executed without password
Cross-reference with GTFOBins for exploitation techniques
Exploitation: sudo vim -c ':!/bin/bash' spawns root shell

output_examples.input: Request Kerberoastable service tickets in Active Directory
output: Tool requests TGS tickets for accounts with SPNs set
Output contains NTLM hashes suitable for offline cracking
Successful crack reveals service account plaintext password

best_practices: Always obtain written authorization before testing on any system you do not own
Document all exploitation steps and clean up artifacts after engagement completion
Use isolated lab environments for learning and testing these techniques
anti_patterns: Never attempt privilege escalation on production systems without explicit client approval
Do not leave persistence mechanisms or backdoors without documented authorization
Avoid running automated tools without understanding their impact on target systems

faq.question: Is this skill legal to use?
faq.answer: Only use these techniques on systems you own or have explicit written authorization to test. Unauthorized access is illegal in most jurisdictions.

faq.question: Do I need special tools for these techniques?
faq.answer: Yes, many techniques require tools like Mimikatz, Rubeus, Impacket, or PowerUp. These must be obtained and deployed separately on your attack infrastructure.

faq.question: Why are some techniques marked as domain-required?
faq.answer: Active Directory attacks like Kerberoasting require network access to a domain controller and valid domain credentials. Local techniques work on standalone systems.

faq.question: What if sudo -l requires a password?
faq.answer: If sudo requires authentication, try other vectors like SUID binaries, cron job abuse, capability exploitation, or kernel exploits depending on your situation.

faq.question: How do I know which technique will work?
faq.answer: Thorough enumeration is essential. Run comprehensive scans with LinPEAS or WinPEAS, then manually verify findings before attempting exploitation.

faq.question: Should I use these techniques in bug bounty programs?
faq.answer: Most bug bounty programs prohibit privilege escalation on their infrastructure. Always check program rules and obtain explicit permission before testing.
